coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of leek-based N,S self-doped CQDs/TiO2 and its application in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants

coredata.dc:description: In this work, N,S self-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs/TiO2) were synthesized from leeks via a microwave method, and a new leek-based N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic material was prepared by roasting. The structural characteristics, optical properties, and electrical properties of the N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composites were characterized by Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Solid ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance (UV–vis-DRS), Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transient photocurrent response, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In addition, the catalytic degradation effect of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) and ibuprofen (IBU). The experimental results showed that the photocatalytic degradation effect of leek-based N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was significantly better than that of TiO2, and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 with 1.86 wt% N,S-CQDs (1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2), which demonstrated optimal photocatalytic degradation. The photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB (20 mg/L) was 94.65 % within 120 min of high-voltage mercury lamp irradiation, which was 41.15 % higher than that of TiO2(53.50 %), and the degradation rate was 80.10 % after four cycles. After 180 min, the photocatalytic degradation rate of IBU (10 mg/L) was 96.92 %, which was 26.39 % higher than that of TiO2 (70.53 %), and the degradation rate could still reach 78.94 % after four cycles. In this work, the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of RhB and IBU by N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were explored by free radical capture and ESR free radical detection experiments. This study may serve as an important reference for enhancing the photocatalytic degradation effect by using biomass self-doped CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials, and as a reference for the photocatalytic treatment of organic pollutants in water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of leek-based N,S self-doped CQDs/TiO2 and its application in the photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, Liaoning, 114051, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Science and Technology Liaoning

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, University of Science and Technology Liaoning, Anshan, Liaoning, 114051, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, N,S self-doped CQDs (N,S-CQDs/TiO2) were synthesized from leeks via a microwave method, and a new leek-based N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic material was prepared by roasting. The structural characteristics, optical properties, and electrical properties of the N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composites were characterized by Scanning electron microscope (SEM), Transmission electron microscope (TEM), X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Solid ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance (UV–vis-DRS), Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), transient photocurrent response, and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In addition, the catalytic degradation effect of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) and ibuprofen (IBU). The experimental results showed that the photocatalytic degradation effect of leek-based N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was significantly better than that of TiO2, and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 with 1.86 wt% N,S-CQDs (1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2), which demonstrated optimal photocatalytic degradation. The photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB (20 mg/L) was 94.65 % within 120 min of high-voltage mercury lamp irradiation, which was 41.15 % higher than that of TiO2(53.50 %), and the degradation rate was 80.10 % after four cycles. After 180 min, the photocatalytic degradation rate of IBU (10 mg/L) was 96.92 %, which was 26.39 % higher than that of TiO2 (70.53 %), and the degradation rate could still reach 78.94 % after four cycles. In this work, the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of RhB and IBU by N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were explored by free radical capture and ESR free radical detection experiments. This study may serve as an important reference for enhancing the photocatalytic degradation effect by using biomass self-doped CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials, and as a reference for the photocatalytic treatment of organic pollutants in water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ibuprofen (IBU) is a common nonsteroidal analgesic and anti-inflammatory drug primarily used to treat pain, inflammation, and fever [1]. Rhodamine B (RhB), a water-soluble synthetic organic dye, is widely used in the textile, printing, dyeing, leather, cosmetics, and food industries [2]. IBU and RhB in aquatic environments pose potential threats to human health [3,4]. IBU can cause chronic toxicity, while RhB is known to have carcinogenic and mutagenic properties [5], causing significant concern, and making the removal of IBU and RhB in sewage imperative. However, traditional sewage treatment methods have difficulty effectively and completely removing these two organic pollutants. Semiconductor photocatalysis technology consists of an advanced oxidation technology that can completely transform organic pollutants into small molecules and water [6,7], with the advantages of utilizing sunlight, mild reaction conditions, and no secondary pollution [8,9]. As a result, this technology has been widely studied in the degradation of pollutants in recent years. Nano titanium dioxide (TiO2) has become a most promising semiconductor for photocatalytic degradation due to its stable chemical properties, non-toxic and safe properties, and low cost. However, its wide band gap, easy recombination of photogenerated electron-holes, and low solar energy utilization limit its practical application [10]. As a result, TiO2 has been modified to improve its photocatalytic activity. Common TiO2 modification methods include metal modification [11–13], non-metal modification [14,15], and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) modification. CQDs are a new type of nanometer fluorescent material with a size of <10 nm. These materials have the advantages of good biocompatibility, unique optical properties, low cost, low toxicity, eco-friendliness, rich functional groups, and high stability [16]. As a result, CQDs have been widely used in semiconductor modification, and the CQDs-modified TiO2 method has been widely reported due to its environmental friendlessness, effective inhibition of photogenerated electron hole recombination, improved spectral response range to visible light region, and significantly improved photocatalytic degradation effect [17]. Numerous studies reported on the degradation of organic dyes by CQDs/TiO2. However, few reports have investigated the degradation of colorless organic pollutants, with no reports on the degradation of IBU by CQDs/TiO2. In this work, leek-based N,S self-doped CQDs were successfully synthesized in one step using the microwave method and used to modify TiO2, and a new leek-based N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic material was prepared. The band gap value of the material decreased, the light absorption range reached the visible region, and the down-state photocurrent was enhanced. In addition, the photogenerated electron transfer rate was enhanced, and RhB and IBU were successfully degraded. Compared to TiO2, the degradation rates of IBU and RhB increased by 26.39 and 41.15 %, respectively. In addition, the photocatalytic degradation mechanisms of IBU and RhB were determined by free radical trapping and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) detection experiments.

Leek was purchased from the campus supermarket, while titanium dioxide (TiO2, 99.8 % metal basis, 60 nm, Jin Hong, Hydrophilic), and absolute ethanol (C2H6O, 99.7 %) were purchased from Tianjin Opusheng Chemical Co., Ltd., Rhodamine B (RhB), ibuprofen (IBU), and potassium iodide (KI) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., while silver nitrate (AgNO3, 99 %) and isopropyl alcohol (IPA, 99.7 %) were purchased from Beijing Chemical Plant. In addition, 1, 4-Benzoquinone (BQ, 98 %) was purchased from Sahn Chemical Technology (Shanghai) Co., Ltd., hydrochloric acid (HCl, 30–36 %) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., and sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 96 %) was purchased from Sinophaceutical Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade without further purification. All tests, experiments, treatments, and washes were performed using homemade distilled water.

The N,S-CQDs were synthesized by the microwave method in one step. First, 5.00 g of washed and dried leeks was weighed and placed into a juicer. Then, 30 mL of distilled water was poured, and the juice was squeezed for 5 min. Clear filtrate was obtained for later use after coarse filtration with gauze and suction filtration using a sand core (0.22 μm). Next, 5 mL of leek juice and 10 mL of water were obtained, placed into a conical flask, and microwaved at 750 W for 12 min to obtain the N,S-CQDs solids. Then, 10 mL of distilled water was used to obtain the N,S-CQDs solutions. The N,S-CQDs emitted blue-green fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet radiation (Fig. 1 (a)). A large number of N,S-CQDs was prepared in 1.9 g/L N,S-CQDs solutions after they were synthesized, dialyzed, rotary steamed, and cold-dried.

First, 1.0 g of TiO2 was mixed with 50 mL of anhydrous ethanol, and the N,S-CQDs solution was added dropwise after ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The solution was then heated in a water bath at 80 °C and magnetically stirred until it evaporated. The evaporated solids were transferred to a crucible, the crucible was placed in a high-temperature drying oven, and heated at 300 °C for 90 min. Subsequently, the material was cooled down to room temperature before retrieving the resulting beige N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic material (Fig. 1(b)), which was ground for later use. According to this preparation method, 5, 10, and 15 mL of N,S-CQDs solutions were added. Composite photocatalytic materials with 0.94, 1.86, and 2.77 wt% N,S-CQDs were prepared and successively denoted as 0.94-, 1.86-, and 2.77-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, respectively.

The 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials and TiO2 (0.10 g each) were precisely weighed and placed in beaker. Subsequently, 100 mL of 20 mg/L RhB solution was added, and ultrasonically dispersed in the dark for 30 min to achieve adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Next, 5 mL of solution was obtained from each of the two beaker and centrifuge (10,000 r/min), and the resulting supernatant was transferred to a cuvette. The absorbance (A0 and At) of RhB before and after dark treatment was measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer at the wavelength of 554 nm. The residual liquid in the beaker was irradiated by a high-voltage mercury lamp (300 W) while magnetically stirred. The absorbance (At) of the RhB solution was measured at regular intervals of 20 min, following the aforementioned procedure, and the degradation efficiency (
η
) was calculated by:

The measurement wavelength was changed to 273 nm, the RhB solution (20 mg/L) was replaced with IBU solution (10 mg/L), the dark processing time was delayed by 60 min, and the above operation was repeated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Exploration of the doping amount of N,S-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was replaced with 0.94-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 and 2.77-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and the experimental operation described in Section 2.4 was repeated.

In this experiment, 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was used as the degradation material, however, the RhB and IBU solution concentrations were changed, and the experimental operation described in Section 2.4 was repeated.

The size and structure of the N,S-CQDs and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were assessed by a transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F20, FEI company, United States) at 200 kV and 120 W. The functional groups on the N,S-CQDs and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 surfaces were determined by a Fourier infrared spectrometer (FTIR, VERTEX 80V, Brooke, Germany), and the crystal structures of the N,S-CQDs and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D8 ADVANCE, Brooke, Germany) with Cu target K (radiation source λ = 1.5418 nm, tube voltage of 40 kV and tube current of 40 mA). The fluorescence properties of the N,S-CQDs were detected by a fluorescence spectrometer (FS5, Edinburgh Company, UK). The morphological structure, constituent elements, and chemical bond types of the N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were determined by a scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss-Sigma 500, ZEISS, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Kratos, UK). The diffuse reflectance and characteristics of the N,S-CQDs/TiO2 surfaces were detected by the integrating sphere method (UV–vis-DRS, UV-3600 Plus, Shimadzu Co., Ltd.). Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMXnano) was used to detect the •OH and •O2 − groups of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 in water under light and dark conditions by the BMPO spin method.

Ag/AgCl was employed as the reference electrode in the three-electrode system, under xenon lamp irradiation, where platinum wire served as the working electrode, and N,S-CQDs/TiO2 and TiO2 were utilized as the co-working electrodes. The transient photocurrent was periodically measured at a switching interval of 30 s. Then the three-electrode system was used for electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) assessment at an open circuit voltage.

Fig. 2 shows the TEM analysis results of the N,S-CQDs, which consisted of quasi-spherical nanoparticles with uniform dispersion and minimal agglomeration. A certain number of N,S-CQDs was randomly selected, and the particle size was measured using Image J software. Particle size analysis was performed using Origin software (Fig. 2 illustration), which indicated that the average particle size of the N,S-CQDs was 6.96 ± 2.28 nm, less than 10 nm. In summary, the N,S-CQDs consisted of carbon nano-materials [18], with good dispersion and a uniform size.

Fig. 3 (a) presents the infrared spectra of the N,S-CQDs. A strong absorption peak appeared near 3370 cm−1, which corresponded to the -NH2/-OH stretching vibration peak, while the absorption peak at 1643 cm−1 was attributed to the N-H bending vibration peak, and the absorption peak at 2373 cm−1 corresponded to the C ≡ N bending vibration peak, which confirmed the successful doping of N in the N,S-CQDs solution [19]. The C-N vibration peak and C-O-C asymmetric stretching vibration peak were found at 1386 and 1052 cm−1, respectively, which confirmed the presence of N [20,21]. The absorption peak at 2960 cm−1 corresponded to the C-H stretching vibration peak [22], and the stretching vibration peak at 655.02 cm−1 was attributed to the absorption peak of the sulfonic acid group (-SO3H), which confirmed the presence of S [23].

Fig. 3(b) shows the XRD spectra of the N,S-CQDs. A broad peak near 2 θ = 22.5°, which corresponded to the (002) crystal plane of graphite carbon, indicated that the N,S-CQDs were carbon materials with a graphite structure. The broad diffraction peak could be attributed to the small particle size, low crystallinity, and high dispersibility of the materials [24]. The small particle size and high dispersibility were consistent with the TEM results.

Fig. 3(c) presents the fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs solutions at different excitation wavelengths (360–470 nm), with an optimal excitation wavelength of 430 nm. A redshift occurred with increased excitation wavelength. This observed phenomenon was possibly attributed to the surface defects induced by the functional groups on the N,S-CQDs surfaces after their modification [25].

Fig. 3(d) presents the fluorescence emission spectrum (Em) and fluorescence excitation spectrum (Ex) of the N,S-CQDs. The peak positions in the fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of the N,S-CQDs were located at 430 nm and 520 nm, respectively. The 90 nm difference indicated that fluorescence emitted by the N,S-CQDs was strong and stable [26]. The luminescence mechanism of the N,S-CQDs could be attributed to the absorption of energy by the surface electrons upon irradiation with excitation light, which led to their transition into an excited state. After returning to the ground state, electrons emitted photons and generated fluorescence through energy release [27].

Fig. 4 (a) shows the SEM analysis results of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials at a resolution of 500 nm. The composite material was entirely in a highly aggregated state, and the particles were quasi-spherical and evenly distributed. Three points were selected in the corresponding scanning area in Fig. 4(a) for EDS point scanning (Fig. 4(g)–(i)). The results showed that the atomic percentages of C in spectrums 1–3 were 8.93, 11.01, and 6.94 %. The percentages of C in spectrums 1 and 3 at the edge were significantly lower than that of spectrum 2. N,S-CQDs could easily recombine with TiO2 on the surfaces of aggregated particles and did not easily bond at the edges of the agglomerated particles.

Energy dispersive spectroscopy (EDS) tests were carried out on the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials. The element images showed that the C, N, S, and other elements were uniformly distributed on the surfaces of the 1.86-N,S-CQDS/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials (Fig. 4(b)–(f)). 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials were prepared [28], which was consistent with the infrared spectrum analysis of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2.

1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was assessed by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope(HRTEM) (Fig. 5 (a)–(c)), and the overall morphology of the composite was observed under low magnification. The samples were uniform in size in a stacked state, where the black areas in the samples were due to the stacking of local samples and the attachment of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2.

Fig. 5(d)–(f) show the high-resolution images of the samples under high magnification, where the lattice stripes of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 could be observed (Fig. 5(f)), and two groups of different lattice spacings were calculated using Image J software. Lattice stripes with a spacing of 0.235 nm corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphite carbon [29], while another set of stripes with a spacing of 0.35 nm corresponded to the (110) crystal plane of rutile TiO2 [30]. Therefore, the N,S-CQDs were compounded onto the (110) crystal plane of rutile TiO2.

Fig. 6 (a) shows the infrared spectra of TiO2, N,S-CQDs, and 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2. For N, S-CQDs, the absorption peaks at 3400 cm−1 corresponded to the O-H and N-H stretching vibration peaks [31], and while the peaks at 1650 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 were attributed to the C-O-C and O-C-O asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration peaks [32,33]. The characteristic peaks at 1060 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of C-O-C [34], and the absorption peak at 650 cm−1 belonged to the C-S stretching vibration peak [35]. For TiO2, the peaks at 3400 cm−1 and 1650 cm−1 were ascribed to the O-H stretching and bending vibrations of the adsorbed water on the sample surfaces [36,37]. The absorption peaks of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 almost corresponded to those of TiO2 and N,S-CQDs in the entire wavelength range, with significant overlap of the three peaks in many places. 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 retained the main features of the N,S-CQDs (e.g., carboxyl and hydroxyl groups), demonstrating that the N,S-CQDs were combined with TiO2.

Fig. 6(b) presents the solid ultraviolet diffuse reflection of TiO2 and 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2. TiO2 demonstrated strong absorption in the ultraviolet region with no obvious absorption in the visible region, while 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 demonstrated strong absorption in the ultraviolet region. However, compared to TiO2, it exhibited obvious light absorption at 400–600 nm. This could be attributed to the fact that the N,S-CQDs in 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 reduced light reflection [38]. N,S-CQDs/TiO2 exhibited photosensitivity, and its synergistic effect with TiO2 could enhance light absorption performance [39].

As shown in Fig. 6(c), the band gap values of TiO2 and 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were 3.50 and 3.36 eV, respectively. The band gap values of the synthesized 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials were reduced, confirming interactions between N,S-CQDs and TiO2 [40].

Fig. 6(d) shows the XRD spectrum of TiO2 and XRD spectrum of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials. The comparison showed that the diffraction peak of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials was lower than that of TiO2. Amorphous N,S-CQDs were compounded on TiO2, which reduced the crystallinity of TiO2 and modified TiO2 [41], indicating that the N,S-CQDs were combined with TiO2.

The valence band (VB) positions of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 measured by VB XPS were located at 2.06 eV (Fig. 6(e)). According to the Eg value and VB position, the corresponding conduction band (CB) potential was calculated as −1.30 eV. The estimated band positions of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 are shown in Fig. 6(f) [42].

Fig. 7(a) presents the XPS spectrum of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalysts, where the peaks at 284.8, 399.36, 459.0, 168.71 and 532.3 eV belonged to C1s, N1s, Ti2p, S2p, and O1s, respectively, which was consistent with the EDS and FTIR results. Fig. 7 (b)–(f) shows the XPS spectra of C1s, N1s, O1s, Ti2p, and S2p, where C1s contained two peaks at 284.8 and 288.04 eV (Fig. 7(b)), which belonged to the C-C/C=C and O-C=O bonds, respectively. Fig. 7(c) shows the N1s spectrum and the peaks at 401.55 and 399.36 eV, which belonged to the C=N, and C-N/N-H bonds.

Fig. 7(d) presents the O1s spectrum, where the three peaks at 532.30, 531.05, and 530.16 eV corresponded to the C=O/C-O-H/C-O-C, Ti-O, and Ti-OH bonds, respectively. According to the XPS spectrum of Ti (Fig. 7(e)), the spin-orbit binding energy of Ti2p1/2 was 464.71 eV, and the spin-orbit binding energy of Ti2p3/2 was 459.00 eV, the oxidation state for both peaks are Ti4+ [43]. The spin-orbit binding energies of Ti2p1/2 and Ti2p3/2 of standard TiO2 were 464.4eV and 458.06eV [44], respectively. By contrast, the Ti2p peak of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 shifted to a high binding energy, indicating strong electronic interactions at the heterogeneous interface of the N, S-CQDs and TiO2 [45]. Fig. 7(f) shows the XPS spectrum of S2p. The two peaks at 169.92 and 168.71 eV corresponded to the orbitals of S2p1/2 and S2p3/2, respectively, which were possibly derived from the sulfonic acid group -CS(O)3-C- bond [35], where S was in a +5 valence oxidation state, consistent with the infrared spectrum analysis results of N,S-CQDs. Therefore, the introduction of N,S-CQDs provided functional groups containing C, N, and S for the composites, which further proved that the N,S-CQDs combined with TiO2 [46,47].

As shown in Fig. 8 (a), the PL spectra of TiO2 and 1.86-N, S-CQDs/TiO2 were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Both samples had a wide emission peak at 550–570 nm [48], while the intensity of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was significantly lower than pure TiO2. These results suggested that the N,S-CQDs effectively enhanced the separation of the photo-generated electron-hole pairs, thus demonstrating the stronger catalytic activity of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 [45,49].

Fig. 8(b) shows the transient photocurrent results of TiO2 and the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composites under high-voltage mercury lamp irradiation after 300 s. Compared to TiO2, the transient photocurrent of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials was stronger, and the separation efficiency of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs was higher [50].

Fig. 8(c) presents the EIS results of TiO2 with 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composites. The arc radius of N, CQDs/TiO2 was smaller than that of TiO2, thus, the impedance of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was smaller, with a faster transfer rate of photogenerated charge and higher photocatalytic activity [51]. This was consistent with the experimental results, where 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 had better effects on the degradation of organic pollutants than TiO2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of N,S-CQDs doping amount on photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: A high-pressure mercury lamp was used as a light source. First, 100 mL of 20 mg/L RhB was photo-catalytically degraded by TiO2 and N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and the degradation effect is shown in Fig. 9 (a). The degradation rates of the 0.94-, 1.86-, and 2.77-N,S-CQDS/TiO2 composites within 120 min were 92.97, 94.65, and 83.67 %, respectively, which were all higher than that of TiO2 (53.50 %) under the same conditions. Furthermore, 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 had an optimal photocatalytic effect, which was 41.15 % higher than TiO2. Fig. 9(b) shows the degradation effect of IBU (100 mL, 10 mg/L) under a high-voltage mercury lamp. The degradation rates of the 0.94-, 1.86-, and 2.77-N,S-CQDS/TiO2 composites were 83.73, 94.65, and 72.60 % within 180 min, respectively, which were all higher than that of TiO2 (70.53 %) under the same conditions. Notably, 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 had an optimal photocatalytic effect, which was 26.39 % higher than TiO2.

As shown in Fig. 9(c), the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of RhB by doping different amounts of carbon dot solutions were elucidated, where the degradation rate constant (k) was calculated based on the slope of the linear graph. The degradation rate constants of 0.94-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and 2.77-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were as high as 0.0218, 0.0258, and 0.0178 min−1, respectively, and these values were approximately 3.69, 4.37, and 3.02 times higher than TiO2 (0.0059 min−1). Fig. 9(d) shows the photocatalytic degradation kinetics of IBU by doping different amounts of carbon dot solutions, where the degradation rate constant (k) was calculated based on the slope of the linear graph. The degradation rate constants of 0.94-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, and 2.77-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were as high as 0.0096, 0.0168, and 0.0066 min−1, respectively, with values approximately 1.75, 3.05, and 1.20 times higher than TiO2 (0.0055 min−1), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pollutant concentration on photocatalytic degradation
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 9(e) and (f) present the degradation efficiencies of the photocatalytic materials at different initial pollutant concentrations. The optimal degradation concentrations for RhB and IBU were 20 and 10 mg/L, respectively.

The stability and reusability of the photocatalyst under the same reaction under a high-voltage mercury lamp were evaluated through cyclic experiments of photodegradation using 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2. After each cycle, the used 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was centrifuged, washed several times with ethanol and distilled water, dried, and then reused. As shown in Fig. 10 (a), the degradation rate of RhB under a high-voltage mercury lamp could still reach 80.10 % after 4 repeated cycles, and 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 showed a high level of recyclability and practicality. As shown in Fig. 10(b), the degradation rate of IBU under the high-voltage mercury lamp could still reach 78.94 % after 4 repeated cycles, and 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 demonstrated a high level of recyclability and practicality.

Table 1 presents a comparison of the photodegradation ability of various reported catalysts for different pollutants, in contrast to the leek-based 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2, which demonstrated better performance than most results obtained in the literature.

BMPO spin-trapped ESR spectroscopy was used to detect •OH and •O2 − in the photocatalytic reactions of the 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 composites in the dark and under illumination for 20 min (Fig. 11 (a) and (b)). The characteristic signals of BMPO-OH and BMPO-O2 − were visible under illumination. Therefore, •OH and •O2 − were the reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation of RhB and IBU by N,S-CQDs/TiO2 [60].

We also performed free radical capture assays. •O2 − [59] was captured b benzoquinone (BQ), •OH [66] with isopropanol (IPA), e− [61] with silver nitrate (AgNO3), and h+ [54] with potassium iodide (KI). As shown in Fig. 11(c) and (d), after BQ addition, the degradation of RhB decreased from 94.8 to 8.1 %, and IBU decreased from 96.9 to 10.4 %. The addition of IPA, KI, and AgNO3 also inhibited the degradation of RhB and BIU, with RhB degradation rates of 23.6, 27.6, and 25.2 %, respectively, and the IBU degradation rates decreased to 20.1, 22.9 and 29.4 %, respectively. Thus, •O2 − acted as an active substance, which played a crucial role in photocatalytic degradation and was the main reactive species, while •OH, h+, and e− were the subactive species[62,63].

The mechanisms of photocatalytic degradation of RhB and IBU by N,S-CQDs/TiO2 were analyzed according to the experimental results and the related characterization of free radical capture and photocatalytic degradation (Fig. 12 ). The electrons in the valence band (VB) of TiO2 jumped to the conduction band (CB) under light irradiation, which resulted in photogenerated electrons e− and holes h+, where e− reacted with dissolved oxygen in water, which produced •O2 −. Meanwhile, the holes h+ interacted with H2O, which produced •OH. •O2 −, h+, and •OH oxidized RhB and IBU and finally produced CO2 and H2O [64,65].

Compared to TiO2, the band gap value of 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was lower. The N,S-CQDs in 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 exhibited electrical conductivity, which could better inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes. More •O2 −, •OH, and h+ were observed, with better photocatalytic degradation rates of RhB and IBU.

In this work, leek-based N and S self-doped CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic materials were prepared by the roasting method. SEM, XRD, UV–vis-DRS, FTIR, XPS, transient photocurrent response, EIS, and HRTEM characterization confirmed that the leek-based N,S-CQDs successfully modified TiO2, broadening the light absorption range of TiO2 to the visible region. The up-state photocurrent was stronger and the transfer rate of photogenerated charge was faster, which effectively improved the separation effect of photogenerated electrons and holes. The experimental results demonstrated that the photocatalytic degradation effect of leek-based N and S-CQDs/TiO2 was significantly improved, and the photocatalytic degradation effect of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 with 1.86 wt% N,S-CQDs content was the best. The photocatalytic degradation rate of RhB (20 mg/L) by 1.86-N,S-CQDs/TiO2 reached 94.65 %, and the first-order kinetic rate constant was 0.0258 min−1, which was 4.37 times that of TiO2 (0.0059 min−1). The degradation rate of IBU (10 mg/L) was 96.92 % within 180 min, and the first-order kinetic rate constant was 0.0168 min−1, which was 3.05 times that of TiO2 (0.0055 min−1). The photocatalytic degradation mechanism of N,S-CQDs/TiO2 was explored through free radical capture and ESR free radical detection experiments. We found that the active species h+, e−,·OH, and •O2 − generated during the degradation process all played a certain role, among which •O2 − was the main active species in the photocatalytic reaction. The leek-based N and S self-doped CQDs/TiO2 composite photocatalytic material demonstrated good stability and could be used many times, serving as an important reference for the photocatalytic treatment of organic pollutants in water.
